data.title: Booking Holdings: Stop Quietly Googling Vacations And Buy This Now
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Many of my friends and relatives have two passive hobbies that they might not even realize are hobbies!

The first is jumping on Zillow (Z) and looking at houses that are for sale in their city or in far-flung cities in America. Many of us receive emails from Zillow regularly, giving us an estimate of what our home may be worth. Even if the reliability of those estimates is questionable, we are still connected to their platform.

The other habit that many of my friends and relatives have is Google searching flights. It's not uncommon for many of us to daydream about a future vacation or a chance to get away. Using these tools regularly gets us plugged into their ecosystem.

As a dividend investor who believes in collecting valuable income from every single aspect of my life, I ask, "How can I collect dividend income or strong capital returns from these habits?"

Today, I want to take a look at the vacation space and a way to benefit from these habits.

Let's dive in!

The Global Travel Titan is on Sale

Booking Holdings (BKNG) is the world's largest online travel agency. Readers are probably familiar with its platforms: the eponymous Booking.com, Priceline, Agoda, Kayak, and OpenTable. For an idea of its size and scale, the company's network includes over 4 million accommodation properties (including 3.5 million homes, apartments, etc.) and around 31 million total reported listings serving over 220 countries and territories in more than 40 languages.

The industry outlook for the global online travel agency market is the first reason I'm optimistic about Booking's future. After all, it's easier to grow a business in a booming industry than in a languishing one. As Kody explained in his recent article, Grand View Research projects that the market will compound by 8.6% annually from $613 billion in 2024 to reach over $1 trillion by 2030. The crux of this growth is increasing smartphone usage and a growing demand for self-service travel bookings.

Booking is strategically doing what's necessary to benefit from this growth. In recent years, the company has been expanding from one travel vertical to multiple travel verticals. This is part of its Connected Trip vision in which customers book accommodations, pre-booked rides and attractions, flights, and rental cars via its platforms. The approach is paying off, with Connected Trip transactions growing at a mid-20% clip in the third quarter, according to President and CEO Glenn Fogel's opening remarks during the Q3 2025 Earnings Call. This vertical now comprises a low double-digit percentage of the company's total transactions.

On the bottom-line side of the equation, Booking's Transformation cost savings program is making progress. The full program is now projected to save $500 million to $550 million annually. This is up from the initial expectations of $450 million.

Throw in share buybacks ($24 billion was remaining under Booking's share repurchase authorization as of Sept. 30, 2025), and this paints a robust growth picture. That's why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to compound at a low-teens to high-teens rate for 2026 through 2028, off an anticipated 2025 base of $227.57.

Booking is a financially stable enterprise as well. It possesses an A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

The 0.7% dividend yield is less than the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.1% dividend yield. The story isn't so much about Booking's starting yield as it is about its dividend growth trajectory. The payout ratio is likely to be in the mid-teens percentage range for 2026. That gives it plenty of room to build on its 1-year dividend growth streak with consistent 10% to 12% annual dividend growth for a Chowder number of around 11 to 13.

Booking is arguably a compelling value right now. At the current $5,348 share price (as of Jan. 7, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19.9 (98% weight for 2026 and a 2% weight for 2027). According to data from FAST Graphs, that's well under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 25.6.

Looking ahead, I believe that the 10-year average P/E ratio continues to approximate fair value for Booking. This is because the company's 15.7% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is comparable to the 10-year average CAGR of 13.4%. That suggests Booking's growth prospects are holding up well, and shares are priced at a 22% discount to fair value ($6,899 fair value per share estimate).

If it reverts to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could deliver a 29% total return by the end of 2026. Through 2030, Booking could log 16%+ annual total returns.

Of course, the company isn't risk-free. The potential for a global recession/global pandemic or possible traffic diversion and margin compression from Alphabet's (GOOGL, GOOG) recent rollout of its AI Mode and Canvas planning tools are standout risks.

As Kody highlighted last month, J.P. Morgan Global Research projects a 35% risk of a U.S. and global recession in 2026. If this happens, the discretionary nature of travel would likely lead many consumers to defer travel. That could put temporary pressure on Booking's adjusted EPS growth rate.

What's more, the COVID-19 pandemic served as an illustration of what a black swan event could do to the company. This is important to note because the Center for Global Development estimates the annual risk of a global pandemic at 2% to 3%. In the next pandemic, Booking's adjusted EPS could drop significantly or even temporarily go negative before quickly bouncing back.

Finally, Google recently launched new ways to plan travel with AI in Search. Travelers can now generate full itineraries and access real-time Flight Deals across 200 countries. Google has also released "agentic" capacities to help Gemini handle tasks, such as beginning reservations and comparing hotel amenities.

While Booking is the primary launch partner for Google's agentic booking, there is a long-term risk of traffic diversion and margin compression. The good news is that the company is offsetting these risks by improving its competitive standing with its Connected Trip vision. This keeps users in its own ecosystem, which mitigates the dependence on Google's search funnel.

Conclusion

Booking is an attractive way to benefit from one of the two quiet hobbies that millions of Americans have. Travel in a recession is always slower, yet Booking can effectively grow its revenue and buy back shares. This will allow greater earnings per share growth than if earnings were growing on their own. Buybacks are essentially a quiet dividend. When I look at a dividend-paying company, I greatly enjoy seeing buybacks that are not only announced but actively being used. This means that the company has excess funds beyond the dividend that they are using to reward shareholders and are laying the foundation for future dividend growth for shareholders who buy and hold, like me.

When it comes to building a portfolio that will provide you with financial security and financial independence, having individual holdings that are expected to provide strong capital returns as well as steady dividend growth are essential building blocks. If you're building a portfolio that is supposed to carry you through the 30-plus years of your retirement, you cannot sacrifice quality for high income today and hopeful income tomorrow. Instead, you must demand that every building block in that portfolio is of the utmost quality. Utmost quality describes the kind of holdings we analyze within Dividend Kings.

data.title: No, We Are Not In A Gold Bubble
data.description:
Both gold and silver have recently touched their all-time highs. That is why many analysts have argued that precious metals, and gold in particular, are in an asset bubble. I personally strongly disagree that the yellow precious metal is overbought. While it is true that buying any asset class at all-time price highs is often a poor idea, we cannot say that an asset that has been treated as money for more than 6000 years could be in a bubble. As I have mentioned in my previous articles, speculative bubbles have existed throughout history. But they have normally lasted for several years or even months. We can talk of the famous tulipmania in the 17th-century Netherlands, radio and car producers’ shares in the 1920s just before the Great Depression, and the dot-com bubble in the 1990s US. All of them burst, leaving many investors penniless. But none of these lasted for centuries. In this article, I will explore why gold is not in an asset bubble.

Upside factors for gold prices

Gold, however, has a very long track record of being a store of value, a medium of exchange, and also a unit of account, thus effectively performing all the functions of money. This is because gold has the key characteristics of money, including durability, portability, divisibility, uniformity, acceptability, and scarcity. Gold is indeed in very limited supply. According to the World Gold Council, “around 216,265 tonnes of gold have been mined throughout history.” So, “if we were to gather all of this gold into a single cube, it would measure approximately 22 meters on each side,” which is very little and is equivalent to a seven-floor house. It is also only ten times higher than a passenger car. This could be seen from the image below.

The supply of physical metal is also much lower than “paper” gold that is traded as derivatives. The paper gold market is around $200-300 trillion, while the physical gold market is only about $11 trillion, which further suggests the physical metal's scarcity.

Higher money supply leads to rising gold prices because the yellow shiny metal is perceived to be an alternative to fiat money.

As can be seen from the graph below, borrowed from Vaulted.com, the correlation between gold prices (yellow) and the USD money supply (green) is very strong.

Interestingly, the money mass in the US (M2) alone neared a whopping $22 trillion at the end of 2025, according to the Federal Reserve. Obviously, this amount is increasing at a staggering pace.

This trend is likely to continue in the future because of the Fed’s easing, widely expected in 2026. According to the Federal Reserve’s minutes published at the end of December 2025, “most participants judged that further downward adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate would likely be appropriate if inflation declined over time as expected.” In other words, the Federal Reserve is quite dovish and expects more rate cuts this year that will further increase the money supply. But the Fed's easing does not only amount to decreasing the interest rates. The Fed also expands its balance sheet by buying Treasuries, which injects liquidity into the US economy, further decreasing the value of the USD and therefore raising the gold prices.

Last year, a law was signed in Texas to recognize both gold and silver as means of payment, thus allowing the two precious metals to serve as mediums of exchange. As I have mentioned above, being a medium of exchange is only one function of money. But gold was partly recognized as money by Greg Abbott, the Texas governor, under the new law. Since gold and silver will soon be legal tender, it will be possible to set gold and silver prices for various goods and services. So, in this way, gold and silver will become alternatives to the USD. In the future, it is likely that more states will follow the example of Texas. The demand for both gold and silver will therefore only rise. But the problem is that both precious metals, especially gold, are in limited supply, as I have mentioned above. The US money mass, however, is growing rapidly. This situation is highly bullish for both gold and silver because there simply will not be enough precious metals to go around.

In addition, it is often said that the BRICS+ countries are moving away from using the USD both as a form of payment and a store of value. Why is that? Well, in my view, this trend became obvious in 2022 in response to the freezing of the $250 billion worth of Russia's foreign reserves held in the European Union. So, many BRICS members decided to move away from using the euro and the USD as forms of payment but, most importantly, as stores of value. That is because many BRICS countries are afraid of losing their foreign reserves held in Western countries, while physical gold held by the national central bankers in their home countries seems to be a much safer alternative.

This trend could be seen from the diagram below.

Moreover, BRICS countries are actively moving away from the USD as a medium of exchange. For many years already have the BRICS members been trading in their own national currencies. As reported by the BRICS Journal of Economics, “China and Russia have entered into bilateral agreements to engage in trade using their own currencies. The BRICS Contingent Reserve Arrangement (CRA) also serves as a framework for providing liquidity and protection against global liquidity pressures without relying on the US dollar.” But this is not the only way to move away from the US dollar. In fact, the alliance is trying to create a common currency that would be backed by gold, a form of the gold standard. As reported by Evrim Ağacı, a Turkish media outlet, BRICS+ “launched a pilot program for a gold-backed currency instrument known as the “Unit.” Released on October 31, 2025, the pilot issued 100 units, each pegged to 1 gram of gold and backed by a mix of 40% physical gold and 60% BRICS national currencies.” In other words, the common currency already exists, but it is still being tested. If its usage rises, then the demand for gold should surge further, thus pushing the prices of the precious metal to new highs.

Valuations

It is often said that gold is far too expensive right now. Indeed, gold is trading at its all-time highs. Even inflation-adjusted prices of the yellow shiny metal are at their all-time highs.

Gold prices

Inflation-adjusted gold prices

But all asset classes are expensive right now, including stocks and property.

Below you can see a graph showing the S&P 500's bull run. The index is also trading at its all-time highs thanks to investors' bets on the Fed's monetary easing.

The same is true of the housing market in the US. The average home price is also close to multi-year highs and is expected to rise moderately in 2026.

So, given the other asset prices, gold is not too expensive.

Below you can see a graph showing the Dow/Gold ratio history. When the ratio is high, for example, when it was at its all-time price highs in 2000 during the dot-com asset bubble, it means that stocks are far too expensive relative to gold. Right now the Dow-to-Gold ratio is nowhere near its all-time highs. However, it is also nowhere near its all-time lows reached in 2011 or 1980, when stocks were much more undervalued relative to gold. I would say that the Dow-to-Gold ratio is close to average, meaning that stocks and gold are fairly valued relative to each other.

Dow/Gold ratio

Also, gold is not too expensive relative to silver, especially after the grey shiny metal’s miraculous rally I will cover in my other articles. When the gold-to-silver ratio is high, it means that gold is very expensive relative to silver, like it was in 2020 or in 2000. But when it is low, it means that silver is overvalued relative to gold, like it was in 1980 or 2011. Right now it is close to average, meaning that gold and silver are fairly valued relative to each other.

Gold/silver ratio

Downside risks

As concerns the downside risks, the main risk I see in the near future is that of a technical correction. These normally happen after brilliant rallies and facilitate further price growth.

But there is also a risk that US macroeconomic indicators would be better than expected. This would prompt the Fed to get more hawkish. But this is not my base-case scenario because inflation is slowing down, while economic activity is cooling.

Also, the lack of geopolitical uncertainty is a bearish factor for gold. But unfortunately, this is not to be expected either. The US-China and US-Russia relations are only getting worse; the situations in Venezuela and Iran are also getting out of control. This should provide further growth potential for gold.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I would say that gold has been in existence for thousands of years while effectively performing the key functions of money, namely as a means of payment, as a unit of account, and also as a store of value. Because the USD’s attraction as the world’s reserve currency gets questioned due to the BRICS+ countries, gold could take its place, thus making the demand for the precious metal surge to new highs.

Investors might benefit from this rally either by investing in ETFs or XAU on the spot market or by buying physical gold. While the former is much cheaper due to the lack of storage expenses, the latter means no counterparty risk. Moreover, the paper-to-physical gold ratio is said to be no less than 100:1, making physical gold look like a much safer option.

data.title: Income Investing Mistakes To Avoid That Can Destroy Your Wealth
data.description:
Introduction

As a dividend-focused investor, I've always wondered why more investors don't focus on collecting income. They instead flee towards growth investing. For me, income investing is a simple and effective strategy that can help with achieving F.I.R.E. It's just usually slower.

I even heard Amazon's (AMZN) founder, Jeff Bezos, speak about this. He mentioned he asked Warren Buffett why don't more people copy his simple investing strategy because it wasn't difficult to understand.

Although Warren Buffett doesn't primarily focus on dividends, he collects billions in dividends each year from quality dividend stocks. It was reported that he received more than $800 million annually from one investment.

Buffett's answer was because his strategy was a get-rich-slowly strategy and that people preferred instant gratification. If you're an income-focused investor, then building a solid income stream to supplement in retirement typically takes years.

I recently wrote an article about the stocks I would buy to position my portfolio if I were starting fresh in 2026. A reader commented, suggesting an article on core principles, risk controls, and return drivers.

As a result, I decided to share personal mistakes I've made along the way as a way to educate others. In this article, I highlight key mistakes income-focused investors should avoid to accumulate wealth.

Don't Chase High Yields

One huge mistake I made when I first started investing was chasing shiny, high yields. One particular investment was Medical Properties Trust (MPW). Although I sold out well before the stock reached a new 52-week low below $4 a share, this still led to a loss.

I invested in MPW due to their essential properties, hospitals. One thing for sure is we need hospitals. This was my thought process when buying. And the fact that the company was highly regarded here on Seeking Alpha a few years ago also played into my decision.

Long story short, investors should be careful not to chase yields. But instead, do their due diligence and understand the associated risks with each company they invest in. Typically, the writing is on the wall before a stock's share price collapses or their fundamentals deteriorate.

As you can see from the chart below, dividend stocks have been a large contributor to market returns.

And while high-yield stocks can have a compounding effect on your portfolio, these should be limited when looking to invest for wealth accumulation. A mistake I made when I first started out.

Don't Ignore Large-Cap Growth

Another huge mistake I ignored was buying and holding high-growth stocks. Although I was collecting a solid stream of income when focusing on higher yields, my portfolio was lagging behind in terms of growth.

Moreover, this slowed down my wealth accumulation process, which is something most investors buy equities for in the first place. While older investors above the age of 60 may focus more on income, it's imperative for younger investors, preferably those aged 50 and below, to add some growth to their portfolio.

You can see from the chart below how different categories of dividend stocks would have impacted your portfolio from 1973 to 2024. Dividend growers like Mastercard (MA) or UnitedHealth Group (UNH) would have grown your $100 investment to close to $16,000 in 41 years.

Initiators like T-Mobile US (TMUS) and Meta Platforms (META), who began paying dividends in 2023 and 2024, would have grown your $100 by the same amount.

This is because MA, UNH, TMUS, and META had strong cash flows before paying out dividends to shareholders. Particularly, TMUS and META were smart in ensuring cash flows were growing & stable before paying out billions to shareholders in the form of dividends.

This is opposite of peer Verizon (VZ), who although has strong cash flows with a payout ratio below 60%, has to obligate a large chunk of cash flow to pay dividends. This gives a company less flexibility for acquisitions, reinvestment back into the business, or paying down debt. Moreover, this impacts their share price.

This is also why TMUS has grown its dividend by double digits the last two years, while VZ has conducted small $0.01 - $0.02 increases. Moreover, this has led to significant outperformance for TMUS in comparison to VZ.

Overlooking Total Returns

Speaking of TMUS and VZ, it's easy for investors to overlook TMUS due to their significantly lower yield. Their current yield sits below 2%, while Verizon's current yield is above 7%.

Again, higher yields have their place inside an income-focused portfolio. But overlooking total returns also destroys wealth over time. In the chart below, you can see the price and total return comparisons for TMUS & VZ from January 1st of 2024 until now.

While Verizon's price return is less than 1%, T-Mobile has a solid return of 17.15%, which would be much higher if not for the share price pullback the company has suffered for the past 10 months or so. In my opinion, TMUS' pullback has nothing to do with the company's fundamentals and more to do with being overvalued.

Looking at the total returns, despite Verizon's significantly higher yield, TMUS has the superior total returns over the same period, up close to 21% to 16.37% for the latter.

Another comparison would be REITs like Agree Realty (ADC) and Realty Income (O). While O is significantly larger and more popular with a higher yield, ADC, in my opinion, is the superior REIT.

This is mainly due to their concentration of investment-rated tenants. Although O has outperformed ADC in total returns in the past year and has performed well in comparison with the S&P (SP500), looking out over a longer period is something investors also overlook.

Over a 10-year period, ADC's total returns are solid in comparison to the index, up approximately 212% compared to roughly 262%. O lags behind at 80.39%.

So, when looking at higher yield stocks, make sure you look for those with solid total returns that either surpass the index or perform well in comparison.

Understanding Taxes

Probably the biggest mistake income-focused investors make is failing to understand how taxes impact their wealth. This helps investors hone in on not only what investments fit into their portfolio but also what accounts these are best suited for.

Different investments like REITs (XLRE) and Business Development Companies (BIZD), because they're required by law to pay out 90% of their taxable income, often pay ordinary income.

This means the distributions you receive will be taxed at a higher rate. Depending on your tax bracket, you can be subjected to up to 37% tax. Companies like META, TMUS, or even Apple's (AAPL) dividends are more tax favorable.

Because their dividends are considered qualified, investors are taxed at the long-term capital gains rate. Meaning between 0% and 20%. While this may not seem like much, the difference between 20% and 37% is extremely significant on hundreds of thousands, or millions, of dollars.

Because of the tax difference, some investments like REITs and BDCs are better held in tax-sheltered accounts like a Roth IRA. Even some covered call funds, or traditional ETFs, pay ordinary income.

Most traditional ETFs pay qualified dividends, while covered call or closed-end funds pay a return of capital. Well-known dividend-paying companies like Enterprise Products Partners' (EPD) distributions are considered return of capital and issue what is known as a K-1 form.

While ROC is more tax advantageous and is the reason covered call ETFs and MLPs appeal to younger investors, these can become difficult at tax time for some.

What ROC means is that taxes are deferred until an investor's cost basis reaches zero. Or the time of sale of the investment. Below is an example:

In reference to K-1 forms, investors should be aware of these typically arriving later in the tax cycle. When I held EPD many years ago, I would usually receive my K-1 form about a month before the tax cycle ends. This is something to also be aware of when looking for potential investments.

Principles & Risk Controls

Wrapping up the article, I wanted to give investors some guiding principles and risks to avoid destroying their wealth. First, cash flow is king. While paying attention to the earnings payout ratio is important, ignoring the free cash flow payout ratio could lead to wealth destruction.

For example, PepsiCo's (PEP) dividends for 2024 and 2025 were covered by earnings, but not cash flow.

Over the last 3 quarters, PEP's earnings have amounted to $5.89, while dividends paid amounted to $4.2675. This appears to give them a safe payout ratio of 72.4% on the surface.

But delving deeper, through 3 quarters, dividends paid amounted to $5.692 billion, while free cash flow only amounted to $2.969 billion. FCF declined nearly 12% from the prior year due to cash from operations decreasing from $6.220 billion to $5.468 billion.

This means PEP is currently paying out more than 100% of its cash flow in the form of dividends. While PEP is a Dividend King who has a good chance to grow cash flows going forward due to recent acquisitions, this puts the dividend at risk.

Starbucks (SBUX) is another example. The exception is that the coffee giant failed to cover its dividends with earnings and cash flow. Earnings for the fiscal year amounted to $1.63, while dividends paid amounted to $2.45.

SBUX paid out $2.771 billion in dividends while free cash flow amounted to just $2.4 billion. While both companies are highly regarded global companies, ignoring red flags can also destroy your wealth.

This is because holding dividend cutters, or those at high risk of cutting their dividend, could lead to underperformance, as these typically experience steep share price drops before and after dividend cuts. And because investors lose trust, this makes it harder for their share prices to recover.

A personal rule of thumb I use is investing in companies with free cash flow payout ratios below 70%. For BDCs and REITs, I like to see dividend coverage above 120% and a payout ratio anywhere below 80%.

Another principle is not to over-diversify. I think a portfolio consisting of 10 to 20 stocks is sufficient. Also, these should include ETFs, but investors should be careful of significant overlap.

I like to see overlap at 30% or below for ETFs. And lastly, try to have a perfect balance when investing. This means not to lean too much to the right on any strategy.

Additionally, look for ETFs with expense ratios below 0.50% for passively managed funds and below 1% for actively managed funds.

Investor Takeaway

Too much income could lead to underwhelming returns. Focusing too much on growth could lead to underperformance because bubbles do pop. That's why it's important to focus on both and know the role of each of your investments in your portfolio.

To wrap it up, manage your expectations. Do you hold the particular investment for income reliability, growth, or both? This helps curb your emotions during market sell-offs or prolonged pullbacks.

data.title: Alger Focus Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025 after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure.

Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top-performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably, burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Focus Equity Fund underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc., Western Digital Corporation, and Natera, Inc. were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Alphabet (GOOGL) is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.
  • Western Digital (WDC) is a hard disk drive (HDD) storage company benefiting from rising hyperscaler data-center investment and the continued proliferation of data. The HDD industry is highly consolidated, with only two scaled manufacturers, and Western Digital holds a leading market position. The business has structurally shifted toward cloud customers as consumer exposure has declined, with cloud representing the majority of Western Digital’s revenue. Importantly, industry participants have emphasized capital discipline—prioritizing higher areal density (i.e., more terabytes per drive) rather than adding significant unit capacity—which supports a healthier supply/demand balance and improved profitability. During the quarter, shares contributed positively as the company reported operating results that validated improving fundamentals, including strong revenue and earnings growth driven by higher unit volumes, better-than-guided drive density, favorable pricing, and lower cost per terabyte that helped expand margins.
  • Natera (NTRA) is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group N.V., Sea Limited, and Meta Platforms Inc were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Nebius (NBIS) is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.
  • Sea Limited (SE) is a global consumer internet company operating three core businesses: Garena, a digital entertainment platform known for developing and publishing online games; Shopee, a leading e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia and Taiwan; and SeaMoney, which offers digital financial services including mobile wallets and payment processing. The company has benefited from strong structural growth in its e-commerce and payments business, along with its gaming business returning to stabilization after years of decline. Shares detracted from performance as investors questioned the pace and durability of Sea’s profitability improvement. Specifically, its largest e-commerce competitor in Latin America lowered free-shipping thresholds, which may increase competitive intensity and pressure margins in the near term.
  • Meta (META) is the world’s largest social media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

data.title: Circle: Dirt Cheap Stablecoin Innovator
data.description:
One of the best times to buy a stock is after a hot IPO cools off following the lockup expiration while the business is still booming. Circle Internet Group, Inc. (CRCL) fits this profile, with the hot stablecoin stock down 75% from the IPO surge to $300. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on the stock based on the ongoing financial market shift to digital assets favoring new internet platforms and the dirt-cheap valuation for the growth rates.

IPO Boomed For A Reason

Circle went public last June, and the stock soared to nearly $300 due to the company running the second-largest stablecoin. The stock fell, as usually happens after a newly public company reports quarterly results picked apart by the market.

The global financial technology company reported Q3'25 revenues surged by 66% to reach $740 million. Circle even grew USDC (USDC-USD) stablecoins in circulation by over 100% during the year to reach 29% of total stablecoin market share, up from only 23% in the prior Q3.

The stablecoin transaction volumes continue to surge with $2.9 trillion in the last quarter, up an incredible 130% YoY to reach 40% of the market. The utility of stablecoins continues to drive volume growth.

The USDC circulation hit nearly $74 billion in Q3 and has reached $76 billion now. Circle has 14% of the USDC circulation on internal platforms versus off-platform at 62%, though the company continues to partner with fintech platforms like Intuit (INTU) and Visa (V) to build the ecosystem out.

A big part of the recent valuation dip is the market probably misunderstanding the revenue generation of Circle is due to interest rates from holding U.S. treasuries for 80% of the stablecoin circulation while the remaining 20% is in cash deposits with the U.S. banking system. The company reported Q3 reserve income of $711 million, partially impacted due to a 96 bps decline in reserve return rate to 4.15%. While it does technically generate revenue from the interest rate spread on money held in reserve to cover stablecoins, the amount of revenue is directly tied to the growth rate of stablecoins in circulation.

For now, Circle produces the majority of the $740 million in quarterly revenues from the interest income generated by the stablecoin reserves. The Other revenue category is growing fast, but the amounts in the Circle Payments Network (CPN) for payments were only $29 million in Q3.

Over time, Circle will better monetize transaction flows and network infrastructure via fees, with network scale providing an advantage. The company has 29 financial institutions enrolled in CPN and is actively reviewing 55 financial institutions for integration in the payments network, while the overall pipeline of financial institutions has swollen to 500.

The annualized payment volume over the last 30 days has already reached $3.4 billion for a product only launched 5 months ago. The company has plenty of avenues with digital assets to move beyond simply collecting reserve income.

Disconnected Price

Circle now trades closer to $70 despite all of the positives for the business. The company is riding the stablecoin wave created by the Genius Act, and the market is forecast to more than 10x the Q3 level at $254 billion to reach $3 trillion to $4 trillion over time.

The stock has seen the market cap dip to only $20 billion, while the revenue target for 2026 is $3.3 billion. The amount seems conservative based on only 21% revenue growth while the company forecasts USDC circulation to grow at a 40% CAGR, and new products are providing major catalysts.

Naturally, the biggest issue is lower interest rates will reduce the reserve rates and dent revenue growth in the short term. Still, Circle only trades at 5.5x 2026 sales targets and a far more attractive 4x 2027 target of $4.5 billion when fewer interest rate cuts are expected, leading to revenues more closely approximating the USDC stablecoin growth.

Circle is already highly profitable with $166 million in adjusted EBITDA during Q3'25. The fintech produced a 57% Revenue Less Distribution Costs (RLDC) margin and a 22% adjusted EBITDA margin. At a 40% RLDC margin in 2026, up from 39% in Q3, the company would produce upwards of $750 million in 2026 adjusted EBITDA based on revenues of $3.3 billion and a similar 57% RLDC margin.

The consensus analyst estimates forecast considerable leverage in the business. Circle is forecast to jump to a 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $960 million.

The stock only trades at 21x 2026 EBITDA targets. Circle sits at the center of stablecoins and the tokenization of digital assets, but the stock trades closer to a market multiple. As interest rates normalize by 2027, the consensus analyst estimates forecast the business growing at a 37% rate, while 2026 EBITDA is forecast to grow 50%, both growth rates warranting a stock trading at far higher multiples.

The biggest risk is that stablecoin adoption doesn't expand beyond current levels. Naturally, the ultimate risk to these stablecoin-focused businesses is anything where the reserves aren't protected, leaving the USDC no longer pegged to the U.S. dollar and redeemable 1:1. The asset would lose all appeal for transactions and payments without the security of retaining value, but this scenario risk appears minimal with the use of U.S. Treasuries.

The other major concern is competition ramping up in stablecoin offerings due to the Genius Act. Up to now, Circle didn't face stablecoin competition from more traditional banks and finance companies, but now the likes of SoFi Technologies (SOFI) have launched offerings like SoFiUSD. Circle has the first mover advantage and established a stablecoin leader like USDC, but new offerings could squeeze future growth rates.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Circle is too cheap based on substantial growth opportunities in stablecoins and digital assets. Investors should use the ongoing weakness to load up on the stock near the post-IPO lows and trading at forward multiples below the growth rates now.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: Macro Recalibration And Valuation Discipline Shape Performance
data.description:
Investor sentiment turned more selective as markets rewarded tangible fundamentals and execution over elevated expectations entering 2026.

U.S. equities moved through a more cautious and transitional period between index selection dates (December 11, 2025 – Jan 9, 2026, the “Period”), as markets closed out the year and began to recalibrate expectations for 2026. While headline indices were relatively stable, underlying market dynamics became more defensive, with investors increasingly focused on valuation discipline, earnings durability, and visibility into near-term returns. Large-cap technology and other leadership cohorts that had dominated much of 2025 faced renewed scrutiny, particularly where optimism around AI investment cycles appeared to outpace evidence of near-term monetization. Against this backdrop, the BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) declined 1.2% during the Period, reflecting its greater exposure to stocks undergoing this valuation reassessment.

Policy and data developments reinforced the more selective tone. The Federal Reserve’s December rate cut was widely anticipated, but accompanying communications suggested a more measured stance heading into early 2026, tempering expectations for additional near-term easing. As the calendar turned, attention shifted quickly to labor market data and early corporate commentary, with investors weighing signs of cooling employment conditions against still-elevated cost pressures. The first week of January further highlighted dispersion across sectors, as technology and digital-asset-linked equities lagged while more defensively positioned areas held up better. Overall, the Period was characterized less by broad macro direction and more by a tightening of standards, with performance increasingly shaped by company-specific execution and the ability to justify elevated expectations entering the new year.

The BUZZ Index returned -2.83% during the month of December compared to a return of 0.06% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index leads the S&P 500 with returns of 31.10% and 17.88%, respectively, as of the end of December.

Rocket Lab Drives BUZZ Gains During the Period; Micron Advances on Tightening Memory Markets

Shares of Rocket Lab (RKLB) were the top contributor to BUZZ Index performance during the Period, advancing on the back of a significant national-security contract that further elevated the company’s role within the U.S. space-defense ecosystem. Rocket Lab was awarded an $816 million contract by the U.S. Space Development Agency under the Tracking Layer Tranche 3 program, calling for the design and manufacture of 18 missile-warning and tracking satellites. The program is focused on persistent global detection of emerging threats, including hypersonic systems, and is part of the Department of Defense’s broader push toward resilient, proliferated satellite constellations. Investors appeared to view the award as both a meaningful revenue anchor and a strong validation of Rocket Lab’s expanding space-systems capabilities beyond launch services, reinforcing confidence in its ability to scale complex manufacturing programs and secure additional defense-related work.

Micron Technology (MU) was the next-largest contributor, continuing to benefit from tightening conditions across global memory markets. Shares moved higher as renewed strength across the semiconductor complex highlighted accelerating demand for DRAM and NAND tied to AI-driven infrastructure buildouts. Industry commentary pointed to rising spot prices, constrained supply, and improving pricing power heading into 2026, reinforcing expectations that memory may be entering a more pronounced upcycle. Micron’s positioning as a key supplier into data centers and AI workloads left it well placed to capture these dynamics, and investors appeared to respond to growing evidence that elevated demand is translating into a more favorable earnings backdrop.

Rigetti and QuantumScape Drag BUZZ Amid Execution and Commercialization Questions

Shares of Rigetti Computing (RGTI) declined during the Period, weighing on Index performance after a period of significant momentum. The stock faced pressure as market participants appeared to recalibrate expectations following its exclusion from the next phase of a high-profile government benchmarking initiative, which may have raised questions about its near-term competitive standing. Combined with a valuation that could be perceived as extended relative to current revenue, the absence of new incremental catalysts may have contributed to a pullback as positioning reset entering the new year.

QuantumScape Corp. (QS) was also a detractor during the Period, retreating despite meeting its annual goal for pilot production equipment installation. The stock’s movement may suggest that expectations may have moved ahead of near-term fundamentals, potentially leading to a "sell the news" dynamic as investor focus shifted toward the multi-year hurdles of commercial scaling. While technical progress and partnership validation remain intact, a breakdown below key moving averages might have accelerated downside momentum during a seasonally quieter trading window.

BUZZ Index January 2026 Rebalance Highlights

Cipher Mining Inc.

Cipher Mining (CIFR) entered the BUZZ Index this month for the first time, reflecting increased investor attention toward operators of large-scale bitcoin mining data centers. Cipher became a public company in 2021 through a merger with SPAC GoodWorks Acquisition Corp, entering during a window when high-growth thematic segments experienced elevated capital inflows. As market conditions tightened through 2022 and 2023, shifting risk appetite and broader pressure on speculative assets weighed on valuations across the sector, including CIFR. More recently, the operating backdrop has evolved as a recovery in digital asset pricing suggested a renewed focus on mining capacity and scalable development pipelines. Cipher has sought to position itself within this landscape, with multiple sites scheduled to come online over the next one to three years. In November 2025, the company announced a 10-year agreement with cloud-computing provider Fluidstack, a contract partially supported by Alphabet (GOOGL). Shares have advanced significantly since early Q4, accompanied by increasing online engagement. CIFR joins the BUZZ Index this month with a 0.79% weight.

Venture Global, Inc.

In January 2025, Venture Global (VG), a leading global exporter of liquefied natural gas, debuted on the NYSE at $25 in one of the largest IPOs of the past five years. The offering raised $1.75 billion and valued the company at approximately $65 billion. At first glance, the transaction appeared to signal a reopening of the IPO market. Beneath the surface, however, signs of strain were already evident. Venture Global initially targeted a $2.3 billion raise at a $110 billion valuation. Within three months, the stock declined to roughly $7 per share, making it one of the year’s most disappointing IPOs. Since then, trading has been volatile, with shares briefly rebounding toward $20 before retreating again toward prior lows. More recently, the energy sector has drawn increased attention and engagement across online forums, potentially reflecting a broader thematic rotation as the advancement of a pro-energy agenda by President Trump appears to be regaining sector momentum. As part of this month’s rebalance, Venture Global enters the BUZZ Index for the first time with a 0.32% weight, alongside Exxon Mobil (XOM) at 0.28% and Chevron (CVX) at 0.58%.

data.title: Eerie And Nervous Calm In Capital Markets
data.description:
There are many moving pieces, and Japan’s finance minister's call for market calm seemed to help. Japan’s 30- and 40-year yields fell around 15 bp today after yesterday’s surge. There is a fragile calm in the capital markets today, and the dollar is mostly consolidating against the G10 currencies. Meanwhile, President Trump will speak at Davos shortly. Also today, the Supreme Court hears the appeal of the president’s attempt to dismiss Federal Reserve Governor Cook.

In an episode that seems to capture the spirit of the American exceptionalism embraced by the Trump administration, Treasury Secretary Bessent, who, alongside the Commerce Secretary and Trade Representative, sat in the front row of the court when it heard the case involving the president’s use of emergency powers to implement the widespread tariffs, chastised Federal Reserve Chair Powell for attending today’s arguments. Meanwhile, the Supreme Court may also give its verdict about those emergency powers.

Prices

G10

• The euro peaked yesterday slightly below $1.1770 in early North American turnover and lost its upside momentum. It slipped back to around $1.1710 and has drifted toward $1.1700 after holding below $1.1735 so far today. Options for 1 billion euros expire today at $1.1690. A break can see $1.1650.

• Japanese bond market stabilized after yesterday’s rout, and the dollar has been confined to about a 20-tick range around JPY158 where $1.25 bln in options expire today. If Japanese officials help contain the bond sell-off, the concern is that it will weigh on the yen. Yet, the softer bond yields have not weighed on the yen.

• Sterling stalled yesterday in front of $1.3500 and by the end of the North American session had given back the lion’s share of its gains. It was sold for almost $1.3400 today. The intraday momentum indicators are stretched, and the $1.3390 area corresponds to a retracement objective of the recent run-up. Below it, initial support may be around $1.3370.

• The US dollar (DXY) was sold to about CAD1.3815 yesterday. It is consolidating in a tight range through the European morning of ~CAD1.3825-CAD1.3845. The 20-day moving average is slightly below CAD1.3810. The CAD1.3860 area may offer resistance.

• The Australian dollar extended yesterday’s gains and reached almost $0.6760 today, its highest level in two weeks, when it poked above $0.6765. That was its best level since October 2024. The high for the day may not be in place, but the A$500 mln in options struck at $0.6800 may be a bit much.

EM

• The Mexican peso remains in demand. The dollar resumed its fall today after consolidating yesterday. The greenback reached MXN17.5320, its lowest level since June 2024. Our next target is around MXN17.38.

• The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at CNY7.0014 today after CNY7.0006 today. Official signals are being closely scrutinized as the CNY7.00 fix level that has been anticipated for some time has been approached. The dollar is consolidating against the offshore yuan in about a CNH6.9535-CNH6.9640 range. Yesterday’s low was slightly below CNH6.95.

• International investor sales of Indian stocks kept the rupee under pressure. The dollar rose to a new record high (~INR91.7450). There are some reports of intervention, but if so, the operation seems to suggest trying to smooth it out rather than reverse the losses.

Other Markets

• Equities are struggling after yesterday’s sharp sell-off. Still, the large markets in the Asia Pacific region were mixed. Tokyo and Taipei fell, but Hong Kong, China, and South Korea advanced. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is off for the fourth consecutive session. It is down almost 0.5%. US index futures are steady to firmer.

• While verbal intervention helped JGB yields push lower after yesterday’s rout, European benchmark yields are mostly slightly firmer. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off a basis point to 4.28%.

• Gold has raced to a new record (~$4888.4) but has pulled back to trade around $4865 in late European morning turnover. Silver is trading quietly and held below yesterday’s record (~$95.88).

• March WTI is little changed near $60.35. It is consolidating in the upper end of yesterday’s range and below the 200-day moving average (~$60.50)

Data

• The US reports September and October construction spending and December pending new home sales. However, the focus is on the Supreme Court. It is “decision day,” and investors are anxious for the ruling on the president’s use of emergency powers to impose widespread tariffs and apparently will use that authority to slap an extra levy on several countries for objecting to his desire to acquire Greenland. Also, the Supreme Court hears the appeal on the president’s attempt to fire Federal Reserve Governor Cook. Fed Chair Powell will reportedly attend the arguments.

• Mexico reports November retail sales. A modest 0.2% increase is expected after a 0.4% gain in October. Mexico’s retail sales rose by an average of 0.2% in Q3 after an average increase of 0.1% in Q2. In GDP terms, consumption contracted in the first half and grew 1.0% year-over-year in Q3 and may have been a little stronger in Q4.

• The UK’s CPI rose by 0.4% in December. It fell by 0.2% in November. The year-over-year rate edged up to 3.4% from 3.2%. The core rate was steady at 3.2%. It is the first increase in the headline rate since last July. Service prices rose 4.5% (4.4% in November). It is also the first increase since last July. Producer output prices rose 3.4% year-over-year, the same as in November. Input price increases slowed to 0.8% from 1.1%. The Bank of England meets on February 5 and is seen standing pat before easing in Q2.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GE HealthCare Technologies Is A Hold Before Earnings (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC) is a healthcare equipment company that I last wrote about in January 2025. In this article I will discuss the upcoming earnings release on February 4th, review my previous trade, and look at the weekly chart using my technical analysis approach to see if GEHC is a buy, sell, or hold.

First, I will review the Seeking Alpha Factor Grades to get an overview of how GEHC is assessed.

Table 1: Seeking Alpha Factor Grades

The valuation grade and the growth grade jump off the page at me, and not in a positive way. Both of those grades are terrible. What is also striking to me is that these grades have been terrible for six months. The valuation grade says that GEHC is overvalued at its current price, and the growth grade says that GEHC has no revenue or earnings growth. That is consistent with being overvalued.

Profitability is outstanding and consistently so. Momentum is decent, and so is the revisions grade. It is noteworthy that the revisions grade has improved drastically over the past six months.

Table 2: Earnings Last Quarter and Upcoming Quarter

Table 2 above shows the earnings summary from last quarter and what is expected on February 4th. Earnings are expected to come in higher than last quarter, and the revenue is expected to be higher as well. You can see how the revisions grade has improved as 12 analysts have increased their earnings estimates while only 3 have decreased their earnings estimates. I see this as bullish.

Table 3: EPS Surprise and Estimates by Quarter

Table 3 shows some reasons for being bullish on GEHC. Earnings estimates for the next four quarters are ahead of the previous estimates based on year-over-year numbers. More importantly, GEHC’s actual numbers have beaten the consensus estimates in each of the last four quarters. If that trend continues, GEHC’s valuations and growth grades should improve, meaning that the stock could see higher prices. My guess is that GEHC will surprise to the upside for earnings on February 4th.

Table 4: Price Target

Table 4 shows a 12% upside price target over the next year. This 12% gain is what one could expect if the earnings over the next four quarters come in as expected. If GEHC continues to surprise to the upside, a more robust 32% gain is possible, at least according to Wall Street analysts.

In January 2025, I wrote about GEHC and stated that I thought it was a speculative buy. I bought shares in the company on January 13, 2025, at $85.00 based on my technical approach that I will outline later on in this article. After a brief run-up in price, GEHC started to decline. I sold my shares on March 31, 2025, at $79.60 using my sell rules. I’m glad I followed my rules, as the stock soon declined a great deal after my trade was closed out.

Now let’s examine the weekly chart.

Chart 1: GEHC Weekly with 30-Wk EMA, Momentum, Volume, and Relative Strength

Chart 1 shows the weekly chart of GEHC over the past 30 months. The stock has gone sideways. I say that because the stock is roughly at the same price today as it was in March 2023, which is the first candle on the left side of Chart 1.

My technical method is to be a trend trader. I want to own stocks that are trending higher. A filter I use to determine trends is the 30-week exponential moving average (EMA). When the stock is trading above an upward-sloping 30-week EMA, I consider that a bullish trend. When the stock is trading below the same moving average, I consider that to be a bearish trend, and I don’t want to own a stock in a bearish trend.

The vertical green line shows my entry into GEHC when I published the earlier article. It was trading above the 30-week EMA, and I considered that to be bullish for the reasons I outlined in the previous article. The vertical orange line shows my exit. And luckily, I got out of GEHC at the top of the big red bearish candle that started at $79.55 and finished much lower at $60.40. I actually got out of the position two weeks too late. My rule is to sell my position when the stock closes the week below the 30-week exponential moving average (EMA). So, as I said, I got lucky being that I should have exited the trade two weeks earlier. Either way, I lost 6.31% on the trade. More importantly, my loss was less than 1% of my account equity.

I have found that my main job as a trader is not to pick winning stocks. My main job is to mitigate risk. I do that by keeping my losses small and not risking more than 1% of my account equity on any one trade. Taking a small loss on any one trade allows me to maintain my capital, and eventually, that capital will be put into a winning trade like this one I recently wrote about. That is how I build my account balance. I take my ego out of the equation, take small losses, and then ride bullish trends higher.

So how does the technical outlook of GEHC look now? Well, it looks bullish. GEHC is trading above its 30-week EMA. The 30-week EMA is rising, which is also bullish.

Momentum shows short-term and long-term bullishness. The Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO) has the black PPO line above the red signal line, which shows bullish short-term momentum. The black PPO line is above zero, which indicates bullish long-term momentum.

The volume picture is generally good. Since the big selloff in late March, early April 2024, GEHC has risen on bullish volume. That means that there are big black volume bars and smaller red volume bars on average. That shows institutional investors have been buying shares since the selloff.

Last week’s volume picture was less bullish, however. GEHC closed 6% lower, and the volume was higher than the previous week’s volume. That is an indication of institutional investors selling their shares. One week doesn’t make a trend, however, but it does pay to keep an eye out for further deterioration of the volume picture.

Relative strength leans bullish. The relative strength line in the bottom pane of Chart 1 is higher now than it was in late October of last year. That means that GEHC has outperformed the S&P 500 index since late October.

My summary of the technical picture leans bullish. It is currently in an uptrend with bullish momentum, volume, and relative strength. Be aware that the uptrend could be vulnerable at the level indicated by the purple rectangle shown on Chart 1. This rectangle connects the previous three highs, which formed a level of price resistance over the last 24 months. That level is also the same as the average projected price target for the next 12 months of $92 mentioned earlier. I don’t own shares of GEHC at this time, but if I did, I would sell them if GEHC closed below its 30-week EMA. Doing so helps preserve capital, which allows you to stay in the game.

data.title: TLT: High Term Premium And Low Credit Spreads Make Long Bonds A Relative Bargain
data.description:
In my last article on the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), I highlighted that high yields on long-term Treasuries reflected the high term premium rather than expectations of higher rates over the long term, which provided an opportunity in the ETF. This thesis remains intact, and considering the extremely low credit spreads on corporate bonds, the TLT is poised to significantly outperform.

The TLT ETF

The TLT has a maturity of 26 years and a duration of 15.5 years, making it highly susceptible to long-term rate expectations. The current average yield to maturity of the fund's assets is 4.9%, which compares with long-term inflation expectations of 2.3%, so investors should expect to receive 2.6% annual returns in real terms. The reason falling long-term rate expectations have failed to bring yields down over recent years is because the term premium has more than offset this impact. This has resulted in the ETF now offering a higher yield than many corporate bond funds, despite the significantly higher liquidity and lower default risk of Treasuries.

Where Bond Returns Come From

The yield on long-term bonds can be broken down into expectations of where short-term rates will average over the long term, plus a premium to compensate for higher duration. Based on the Adrian, Crump, and Moench 10-year estimate and the Bloomberg estimate of the UK 30-year term premium, we can infer that the US 30-year term premium is around 2%. This means that over the next 30 years, long-term Treasuries are expected to outperform cash by at least 2% annually. This is very high from a historical perspective and has been justified by the US' rising debt load, which has created uncertainty over the government's ability to repay its debt over the long term.

Relative Value

However, the government can always rely on the Federal Reserve to buy its bonds in the case that lack of private demand threatens a debt default. If policymakers were to allow a default, then it would likely blow up the entire global debt market and cause huge default rates across the corporate and other debt markets.

It is surprising then that these credit markets offer such low yields. As shown below, 30-year Treasuries now yield significantly more than short-term high-grade US corporates and are on par with mid-term high-grade US corporates.

Meanwhile, the spread of long-term high-grade corporates over long-term Treasuries has fallen to its lowest level since the late 1990s at just 80bps. This is around half of the long-term average and a full standard deviation below it.

A Bet Against Complacency

It is tough to envisage a scenario where long-term Treasuries underperform mid-term corporates for an extended period from current levels. Corporate bonds are subject to default risk and liquidity risk and are subject to state taxes, unlike Treasuries, so their yields should be naturally higher. Currently low credit spreads reflect high levels of complacency about the state of the economy, which has allowed investors to focus on the upside of marginally higher returns in a positive economic scenario rather than liquidity and default risk.

However, in the event of a shift in market sentiment, Treasuries could outperform significantly as investors decide current wafer-thin spreads are not worth the risk, causing a rush into the safety of Treasuries. Long-term Treasuries outperformed long-term investment-grade corporates by almost 50% during the global financial crisis and 70% during the Covid crisis. While another such risk event does not appear to be on the horizon, they rarely appear to be until yield spreads have already blown out and Treasuries have already outperformed significantly.

Summary

The TLT ETF is now extremely attractive relative to corporate bonds. With a term premium estimated to be around 2%, investors should now expect to see 2% annual outperformance relative to cash and 2.6% annual real returns after inflation. This is now higher than the expected return on many corporate bond funds despite the much higher liquidity and asymmetric upside risks in the event of a deterioration in risk appetite.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Turning The Corner As Fundamentals Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Today, we are analyzing STMicroelectronics (STM). Here at the Lab, we view the setup as constructive, even given STM's underperformance over the past 12 months (Fig. 1), which lagged peers such as Microchip (+30%) and Infineon (+28%). In our view, Microchip's recent preannouncements, particularly relevant given its industrial MCU positioning, point to improving near-term conditions that should also be supportive of STM. This is not the first time that we have covered STMicroelectronics, and our neutral stance was backed by multiple factors, including a pricey valuation, Depressed Earnings Ahead, and weak automotive demand. Additionally, we reported our note titled 3 Risks To Price, highlighting negative FX, Mobileye Global's inventory build-up, and Apple concentration. Our assessment of several of the risks outlined above has evolved, which makes this an appropriate time to reassess and update our forecast.

Fig. 1

Why Are We Positive?

Heading into Q4 2025 and looking at 2026, we see AI-led demand continuing to underpin sales growth. Thanks to our cross-read on Infineon, we see an Underappreciated AI Opportunity in STM as well. Additionally, the analog segment shows early signs of a cyclical upswing. Analog products, MEMS, and Sensors (AM&S) account for 45% of STM's current sales (Fig. 2). Against this backdrop, we prefer to focus on names that have lagged despite improving fundamentals. ASML and STM fit this profile and remain our top picks. Additionally, the EU semi-cap names have also materially underperformed their US counterparts. We might see favour capital recycling activities within the sector.

Fig. 2

The company guides Q4 top-line sales up 2.9%, below the usual ~7% seasonal uplift. This reflects the digestion of industrial channel inventory and ongoing automotive destocking. That said, taking advantage of our cross-coverage within the auto industry, we reported how "lobbying efforts by the European auto industry for more flexible post-2030 CO₂ targets." In our view, there was "a strong chance that the long-term CO₂ framework for carmakers will be eased," and this is what has happened. STM's main clients are in the Automotive segment (Fig. 2), which supports sales and margins.

In a previous analysis, we reported Apple and Mobileye Global client concentration risks. But Q2 2026 should follow typical seasonality; however, the company implied a +16% yearly growth. This will likely support margin expansion via operating leverage. We believe Apple launches will translate into higher volumes. This will lead to gradual improvement in both top-line sales and gross margin through 2026. We also see meaningful company-specific tailwinds next year, most notably Tesla and Mobileye, where easier comps could translate into differentiated growth.

Looking at the Q3 release, STM operating expenses were the main negative, with Q4 OPEX guided at ~$915 million, up from the ~$840 million recorded in Q3 (Fig. 3). We need to report that management has introduced cost actions. With easier FX, we expect some benefits in 2026. Even if we anticipate flat OPEX year over year, versus prior consensus expectations for a low- to mid-single-digit decline, we believe the company will be back to profitable growth. Here at the Lab, we read the expected ~180 basis points improvement in Q4 gross margin as encouraging (Fig. 4). A solid balance sheet and stable inventory level also support this.

Fig. 3

Fig. 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last update (August 2025), STM valuation was expensive, with a P/E ratio above 30x. Since then, the company's EPS increased considerably on a quarterly basis (Fig. 5), and even if it still lags behind 2023/2024 results, we see light at the end of the tunnel. The company's market cap is €21 billion with a favorable net financial position of €2.6 billion. Additionally, to optimise STM strategic priorities, the company reduced its CAPEX investment, and we believe this will also be the 2026 trend. Looking toward 2026, we see a higher gross margin at 35.7%. This is an improvement over the 33.8% estimate in 2025. Even accounting for a slight negative FX, we see positive impacts from manufacturing efficiency, cost-saving initiatives, and the optimisation of unused capacity. Rolling forward our estimates, we forecast sales at €13.6 billion. This automotive positive momentum will persist into 2026, reinforcing our optimistic view on the analog complex. Going down to the P&L, we see EBIT at the 10% level. This is still well below the 2023-2024 numbers, but given lower CAPEX investment and a favorable financial position, we project net income of €1.2 billion. In our calculation, we used a 16% tax rate (aligned with the company's previous tax rate and included a favorable interest rate). With 911 million outstanding shares, STM's EPS is set at €1.31. This implied a P/E of 17.5x and an EV/EBITDA of 6x. Applying a 20x P/E (Fig. 6), aligned with STM's historical average, we derive a target price of €26.2 per share ($30.5 in ADR). We have not adjusted our valuation for the current cash and cash equivalents, and we also report a valuation cap compared to peers (which we believe is justified). That said, we moved our rating from neutral to buy.

Fig. 5

Fig. 6

Risks

Key downside risks include a larger-than-expected unused industrial capacity. The company is progressing with restructuring actions similar to those seen in Q1-Q2 2025. We believe additional negative one-offs will be taken negatively by Wall Street. Here at the Lab, despite a more benign view on Auto, we still see a challenging environment. We need to report a weaker backdrop in consumer electronics (Apple). Slower innovation and CAPEX coming above expectations are downsides to consider. Here at the Lab, we are closely monitoring inventory trends, and in the near term, STM pricing could remain under pressure. We also report a weakening of the $/€ exchange rate, and the medium-term risk centres on STM's ability to deliver new image-sensing products for smartphones.

Conclusion

Here at the Lab, we have consistently reported downside risks from STM. At this stage, the company will enter a favorable period due to easier comps and a positive underlying sector that is already covered. With a valuation still at a discount to peers, this might be a positive momentum to step in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
